q: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
question: The value of the acceleration due to gravity is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a height <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>h</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mi>R</mi><mn>2</mn></mfrac></math> (<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>R</mi><mo>=</mo></math> radius of the earth) from the surface of the earth. It is again equal to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>g</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> at a depth <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> below the surface the earth. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mfrac><mi>d</mi><mi>R</mi></mfrac></mfenced></math> equals:
solution:
Given that

gn=gd

GM(R+h)2=GMR3(R-d)

GM(R+R/2)2=GMR3(R-d)

4GM9R2=GMR21-dR

49=1-dR

dR=1-49=59

d=59R

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Knowing initial position $x_0$ and initial momentum $p_0$ is enough to determine the position and momentum at any time $t$ for a simple harmonic motion with a given angular frequency $\omega$.
Reason (R): The amplitude and phase can be expressed in terms of $x_0$ and $\mathrm{p}_0$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements. One is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Knowing initial position $x_0$ and initial momentum $p_0$ is enough to determine the position and momentum at any time $t$ for a simple harmonic motion with a given angular frequency $\omega$.
Reason (R): The amplitude and phase can be expressed in terms of $x_0$ and $\mathrm{p}_0$.
In the light of the above statements, choose the correct answer from the options given below :
options: Both (A) and (R) are true but (R) is NOT the correct explanation of (A)
Both $(\mathbf{A})$ and $(\mathbf{R})$ are true and $(\mathbf{R})$ is the correct explanation of $(\mathbf{A})$
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { If we express position } x(t)=A \sin (\omega t+\phi) \\ & \text { then } x_0=A \sin \phi \\ & v_0=A \omega \cos \phi \\ & \Rightarrow \tan \phi=\frac{\omega x_0}{v_0} \\ & A=\sqrt{x_0^2+\frac{v_0^2}{\omega^2}}\end{aligned}$
Hence both position and linear momentum of a particle can be expressed as a function of time if we know initial momentum and position

Number of moles of oxygen O2=12832=4

Using ideal gas equation, PV=nRT,

Where pressure P=1 atm=1.01×105 Pa STP

Putting the values, 

1.01×105 V=8+4×8.314×273

V=12×8.314×2731.01×105 m3

=0.27×106 cm3=27×104 cm3

subject: Physics
q: A tuning fork vibrates with frequency $256 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and gives one best per second with the third normal mode of vibration of an open pipe. What is the length of the pipe? (Speed of sound of air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
question: A tuning fork vibrates with frequency $256 \mathrm{~Hz}$ and gives one best per second with the third normal mode of vibration of an open pipe. What is the length of the pipe? (Speed of sound of air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ )
solution:
According to question, tuning fork gives 1 beat/second with (N) $3^{\text {rd }}$ normal mode. Therefore, organ pipe will have frequency $(256 \pm 1) \mathrm{Hz}$. In open organ pipe, frequency $\mathrm{n}=\frac{\mathrm{NV}}{2 \ell}$
or, $255=\frac{3 \times 340}{2 \times \ell} \Rightarrow \ell=2 \mathrm{~m}=200 \mathrm{~cm}$

subject: Physics
q: A beam of light consisting of wavelengths 650 nm and 550 nm illuminates the Young's double slits with separation of 2 mm such that the interference fringes are formed on a screen, placed at a distance of 1.2 m from the slits. The least distance of a point from the central maximum, where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}$.
question: A beam of light consisting of wavelengths 650 nm and 550 nm illuminates the Young's double slits with separation of 2 mm such that the interference fringes are formed on a screen, placed at a distance of 1.2 m from the slits. The least distance of a point from the central maximum, where the bright fringes due to both the wavelengths coincide, is $\_\_\_\_$ $\times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}$.
solution:
Given wavelengths are $\lambda_1 = 650$ nm and $\lambda_2 = 550$ nm.
Slit separation $d = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m.
Distance to screen $D = 1.2$ m.
For the bright fringes of both wavelengths to coincide at a distance $y$ from the central maximum, we have:
$y = n_1 \frac{\lambda_1 D}{d} = n_2 \frac{\lambda_2 D}{d}$
where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are integers representing the order of the fringes.
This simplifies to $n_1 \lambda_1 = n_2 \lambda_2$.
$\frac{n_1}{n_2} = \frac{\lambda_2}{\lambda_1} = \frac{550}{650} = \frac{11}{13}$
The least values for coincidence are $n_1 = 11$ and $n_2 = 13$.
The least distance $y$ is given by:
$y = n_1 \frac{\lambda_1 D}{d} = 11 \times \frac{650 \times 10^{-9} \times 1.2}{2 \times 10^{-3}}$
$y = 11 \times 650 \times 0.6 \times 10^{-6}$ m
$y = 11 \times 390 \times 10^{-6}$ m
$y = 4290 \times 10^{-6}$ m
$y = 429 \times 10^{-5}$ m.

q: In order to determine the Young's Modulus of a wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>) and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>), a weight of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) was hanged to get the elongation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>). What will be the fractional error in the value of Young's Modulus determined by this experiment?
question: In order to determine the Young's Modulus of a wire of radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>) and length <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math>  (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math>), a weight of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> ) was hanged to get the elongation of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> (measured using a scale of least count <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>001</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>). What will be the fractional error in the value of Young's Modulus determined by this experiment?
solution:
Y=StressStrain=FLAl=mgLπR2l

ΔYY=Δmm+ΔLL+2ΔRR+Δll

ΔYY×100=10011000+11000+20.0010.2+0.0010.5

=110+110+1+15=1410=1.4%

subject: Physics
q:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=57 \cos \left[7.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{t}-5 \times 10^{-3}(3 x+4 y)\right](4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}) N / C$.
The associated magnetic field in Tesla is
question:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave in free space is $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}=57 \cos \left[7.5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{t}-5 \times 10^{-3}(3 x+4 y)\right](4 \hat{i}-3 \hat{j}) N / C$.
The associated magnetic field in Tesla is
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{K}}=3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{K}}=\frac{3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+4 \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{5} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{E}}=\frac{4 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}}{5} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{~B}}=\hat{\mathrm{K}} \times \hat{\mathrm{E}} \\ & \hat{\mathrm{B}}=-\hat{\mathrm{Z}} \\ & \mathrm{B}_0=\frac{\mathrm{E}_0}{\mathrm{C}}=\frac{57}{3 \times 10^8}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
question: The energy released if hydrogen atoms are combined to form $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ is __________ MeV. (Take binding energies per nucleon of $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ and $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ as $1.1$ MeV and $7.2$ MeV, respectively)
solution:
The fusion reaction is given by:

$2 ^{2}_{1}\text{H} \rightarrow ^{4}_{2}\text{He}$

Binding energy of one $^{2}_{1}\text{H}$ nucleus = $2 \times 1.1 = 2.2$ MeV

Total initial binding energy = $2 \times 2.2 = 4.4$ MeV

Binding energy of $^{4}_{2}\text{He}$ nucleus = $4 \times 7.2 = 28.8$ MeV

Energy released $\Delta E$ = Final binding energy - Initial binding energy

$\Delta E = 28.8 - 4.4 = 24.4$ MeV

Answer: $24.4$

subject: Physics
q: A slab is subjected to two forces $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ of same magnitude $F$ as shown in the figure. Force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ is in XY- plane while force $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ acts along $z$ -axis at the point $(2 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j})$. The moment of these forces about point $\mathrm{O}$ will be:
question: A slab is subjected to two forces $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{1}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ of same magnitude $F$ as shown in the figure. Force $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}_{2}}$ is in XY- plane while force $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ acts along $z$ -axis at the point $(2 \vec{i}+3 \vec{j})$. The moment of these forces about point $\mathrm{O}$ will be:
solution:
Given, $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{1}=\frac{\mathrm{F}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\frac{\mathrm{F} \sqrt{3}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})$

$\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{1}=0 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+6 \hat{\mathrm{j}}$

Torque due to $\mathrm{F}_{1}$ force

$$

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{1}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}_{1} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{F}}_{1}=6 \hat{\mathrm{j}} \times\left(\frac{\mathrm{F}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{i}})+\frac{\mathrm{F} \sqrt{3}}{2}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})\right)=3 \mathrm{~F}(\hat{\mathrm{k}})

$$

Torque due to $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ force

$$

\begin{aligned}

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{2}}=(2 \hat{\mathrm{i}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{j}}) \times & \mathrm{F} \hat{\mathrm{k}}=3 \mathrm{~F} \hat{\mathrm{i}}+2 \mathrm{~F}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}}) \\

\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{net}}=\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{1}}+\vec{\tau}_{\mathrm{F}_{2}} &=3 \mathrm{Fi}+2 \mathrm{~F}(-\hat{\mathrm{j}})+3 \mathrm{~F}(\hat{\mathrm{k}}) \\

&=(3 \hat{\mathrm{i}}-2 \hat{\mathrm{j}}+3 \hat{\mathrm{k}}) \mathrm{F}

\end{aligned}

$$

subject: Physics
q:
A solid cylinder of mass m is wrapped with an inextensible light string and, is placed on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. The frictional force acting between the cylinder and the inclined plane is:

[The coefficient of static friction, μs, is 0.4 ]
question:
A solid cylinder of mass m is wrapped with an inextensible light string and, is placed on a rough inclined plane as shown in the figure. The frictional force acting between the cylinder and the inclined plane is:

[The coefficient of static friction, μs, is 0.4 ]
solution:
Let's take solid cylinder is in equilibrium

T+f=mgsin60

TR-fR=0

Solving we get

T=freq=mgsinθ2

But limiting friction < required friction

μmgcos60°<mgsin60°2

∴ Hence cylinder will not remain in equilibrium

Hence f= kinetic

=μkN

=μkmgcos60°

=mg5

subject: Physics
q: Two radioactive materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> have decay constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of A will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>" after a time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is _____ .
question: Two radioactive materials <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> have decay constants <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>25</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>16</mn><mi>λ</mi></math> respectively. If initially they have the same number of nuclei, then the ratio of the number of nuclei of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> to that of A will be "<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>e</mi></math>" after a time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mi>a</mi><mi>λ</mi></mrow></mfrac></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>a</mi></math> is _____ .
solution:
According to radioactive decay formula, N=N0e-λt, here, N0 is the initial number of nuclei in a radioactive material.

Given here,  λ1=25λ and λ2=16λ.

And NBNA=e

For radioactive material A, NA=N0e-λ1t

For radioactive material B, NB=N0e-λ2t

Thus, the ratio NBNA=e-λ2te-λ1t=e-λ2t·eλ1t

⇒e1=eλ1-λ2t

⇒λ1-λ2t=1

Time, t=1λ1-λ2=125λ-16λ=19λ.

Hence, the value of a=9.

subject: Physics
q: A bar magnet has total length $2 l=20$ units and the field point $P$ is at a distance $\mathrm{d}=10$ units from the centre of the magnet. If the relative uncertainty of length measurement is $1 \%$, then uncertainty of the magnetic field at point P is :
question: A bar magnet has total length $2 l=20$ units and the field point $P$ is at a distance $\mathrm{d}=10$ units from the centre of the magnet. If the relative uncertainty of length measurement is $1 \%$, then uncertainty of the magnetic field at point P is :
solution: Magnetic field at $P, B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{m(2 l)}{r^3}$, where $m$ is the pole strength $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow\left(\frac{\Delta B}{B} \times 100\right) & =\left(\frac{\Delta l}{l}\right) \times 100+3\left|\frac{\Delta r}{r}\right| \times 100 \\ & =1 \%+3 \%=4 \% \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: A set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> tuning forks is arranged in a series of increasing frequencies. If each fork gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> beats with respect to the preceding fork and the frequency of the last fork is twice the frequency of the first, then the frequency of last fork is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
question: A set of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn></math> tuning forks is arranged in a series of increasing frequencies. If each fork gives <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> beats with respect to the preceding fork and the frequency of the last fork is twice the frequency of the first, then the frequency of last fork is _____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>Hz</mi></math>.
solution:
Each fork produces 4 beats per second with the previous means each fork has frequency 4 Hz more than the previous.

Using relation, flast=ffirst+N-1x, here, N is the number of tuning fork in series and x is beat frequency between two successive forks.

2f0=f0+ 20-14

⇒2f0=f0+76⇒f0=76 Hz

Frequency of last tuning fork is 152 Hz.

subject: Physics
q: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
question: Modern vacuum pumps can evacuate a vessel down to a pressure of <math> <mrow> <mtext>4.0</mtext> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> <mn>5</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math> atm. at room temperature (300 K). Taking R = 8.3 JK<sup>-1</sup>  mole<sup>-1</sup>, 1 atm = 10<sup>5</sup> Pa and <math> <mrow> <msub> <mtext>N</mtext> <mtext>Avogadro</mtext> </msub> <mo>=</mo> <mn>6</mn> <mo>×</mo> <mn>1</mn> <msup> <mn>0</mn> <mrow> <mn>2</mn> <mn>3</mn> </mrow> </msup> <msup> <mo>mole</mo> <mrow> <mo>-</mo> <mn>1</mn> </mrow> </msup> </mrow> </math>, the mean distance between molecules of gas in an evacuated vessel will be of the order of :
solution:
Let the inter molecular distance be D
Then in a volume of 4 3 π D 3 there is only one molecule.

4 3 π D 3 P=( 1 N A )RT

D= ( 3RT 4π N A P ) 1 3

Substituting P=4× 10 −10 Pa,  R=8.3,   N A =6× 10 23 ,  T=300K

D=0.2 mm

subject: Physics
q: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
question: The de-Broglie wavelength associated with the electron in the n=4 level is:
options: Half of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Four times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
<math><mfrac><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow><mrow><mn>4</mn></mrow></mfrac><mi>t</mi><mi>h</mi></math>  of the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
Two times the de-Broglie wavelength of the electron in the ground state
solution:
De-Broglie wavelength is given as

λ=hp

∵p22m=k⇒p=2mk

⇒λ=h2mk

At ground level energy =E0 so

At n=4 level energy =E0n2=E016

∴ at ground level

λ0=h2m E0

At n = 4 level

λ′=h2mE016=4h2m E0

⇒λ′=4λ0

subject: Physics
q: The amplitude of wave disturbance propagating in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math> at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> are in metres. The shape of wave does not change during the propagation. The velocity of the wave will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
question: The amplitude of wave disturbance propagating in the positive <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math>-direction is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mo>(</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mi>x</mi><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math> at time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>+</mo><mo>(</mo><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><msup><mo>)</mo><mn>2</mn></msup></mrow></mfrac></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>t</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>s</mi></math>, where <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>y</mi></math> are in metres. The shape of wave does not change during the propagation. The velocity of the wave will be <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math>
solution:
At t=0,y=11+x2
At time t=t,y=11+(x-vt)2
At t=1,y=11+(x-v)2 …(i)
At t=1,y=11+(x-2)2 …(ii)
Comparing (i) and (ii)
v=2 m s-1

taking log & then differentiate

subject: Physics
q:
A coil is placed in magnetic field such that plane of coil is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field. The magnetic flux through a coil can be changed:

A. By changing the magnitude of the magnetic field within the coil.

B. By changing the area of coil within the magnetic field.

C. By changing the angle between the direction of magnetic field and the plane of the coil.

D. By reversing the magnetic field direction abruptly without changing its magnitude.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
question:
A coil is placed in magnetic field such that plane of coil is perpendicular to the direction of magnetic field. The magnetic flux through a coil can be changed:

A. By changing the magnitude of the magnetic field within the coil.

B. By changing the area of coil within the magnetic field.

C. By changing the angle between the direction of magnetic field and the plane of the coil.

D. By reversing the magnetic field direction abruptly without changing its magnitude.

Choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below:
solution:
Magnetic flux through a coil is given by ϕ=B→.A→=BAcosθ, where,Bis magnetic field, A is area of coil and θ is the angle between B and A.

Thus, from above expression, we can say that magnetic flux can be changed by changing magnetic field, area of coil, change the angle between B and A.

Hence, correct options are A, B and C.

subject: Physics
q: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
question: In a series <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>L</mi><mi>R</mi></math> circuit <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mi>R</mi></math> and power factor of the circuit is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math>. When capacitor with capacitance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>C</mi></math> such that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>L</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><msub><mi>X</mi><mi>C</mi></msub></math> is put in series, the power factor becomes <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. The ratio <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><msub><mi>P</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mfrac></math> is
solution:
In case of L-R circuit

Z=XL2+R2 Therefore, power factor

P1=cosϕ=RZ

As XL=R ⇒Z=2R

⇒P1=R2R⇒P1=12

In case of  L-C-R circuit

Z=R2+XL-XC2

According to the question XL=XC ⇒Z=R  ⇒P2=cosϕ=RR=1

⇒P1P2=12

subject: Physics
q: If velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math> are chosen as the base quantities, the dimensions of the mass will be :
question: If velocity <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>V</mi></mfenced></math> time <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>T</mi></mfenced></math> and force <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]"><mi>F</mi></mfenced></math> are chosen as the base quantities, the dimensions of the mass will be :
solution:
[M]1=[V]a[T]b[F]c
[M]1=LT-1a[T]bMLT-2c
[M]1=[M]c[L]a+c[T]-a+b-2c
Thus,

c=1,a+c=0 andb-a-2c=0⇒a=-1⇒b=-1+2=1
Thus, [M]=[V]-1[ T]1[ F]1

q: A heat engine operates with the cold reservoir at temperature 324 K. The minimum temperature of the hot reservoir, if the heat engine takes 300 J heat from the hot reservoir and delivers 180 J heat to the cold reservoir per cycle, is _____  K.
question: A heat engine operates with the cold reservoir at temperature 324 K. The minimum temperature of the hot reservoir, if the heat engine takes 300 J heat from the hot reservoir and delivers 180 J heat to the cold reservoir per cycle, is _____  K.
solution:
Using the relation, Q2Q1=T2T1

Or  180300=324T1⇒T1=324×300180=540 K

q: In the figure below, P and Q are two equally intense coherent sources emitting radiation of wavelength 20m. The separation between P and Q is 5m and the phase of P is ahead of that of Q by 90°.A,B and C are three distinct point of observation, each equidistant from the midpoint of PQ. The intensities of radiation at A,B,C will be in the ratio :
question: In the figure below, P and Q are two equally intense coherent sources emitting radiation of wavelength 20m. The separation between P and Q is 5m and the phase of P is ahead of that of Q by 90°.A,B and C are three distinct point of observation, each equidistant from the midpoint of PQ. The intensities of radiation at A,B,C will be in the ratio :
solution:
ΔPA=π2×202π-5=0   so ΔϕA=0

ΔPB=π2×202π=5   so ΔϕB=π2

ΔPC=5+5=10   so ∆ϕc=π

I=locos2Δϕ2

 so IA=I0

IB=I02

 Ic =0

so  IA :IB:IC=2:1:0

subject: Physics
q: Consider a sphere of radius R which carries a uniform charge density ρ . If a sphere of radius R2 is carved out of it, as shown, the ratio EA→EB→ of magnitude of electric field EA→ and EB→ , respectively, at points A and B due to the remaining portion is:
question: Consider a sphere of radius R which carries a uniform charge density ρ . If a sphere of radius R2 is carved out of it, as shown, the ratio EA→EB→ of magnitude of electric field EA→ and EB→ , respectively, at points A and B due to the remaining portion is:
solution:
E=ρr3ε0
EA=-ρR23ε0
EA=ρR6ε0
Electric filed at point B=EB=E1A+E2A
E1A= Electric Filed Due to solid sphere of radius R at point B=ρR3ε0
E2A= Electric Filed Due to solid sphere of radius R2 (which having charge density –ρ ) at point B
=-KQ'×49R2=-ρR54ε0
EB=E1A+E2A=ρR3ε0-ρR54ε0=17ρR54ε0
EAEB=917

subject: Physics
q:
A light beam of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a metal having work function of 1.25 eV, placed in a magnetic field of intensity B. The electrons emitted perpendicular to the magnetic field B, with maximum kinetic energy are bent into a circular arc of radius 30 cm. The value of B is ________×10-7 T.
Given hc=20×10-26 J m, the mass of the electron=9×10-31 kg.
question:
A light beam of wavelength 500 nm is incident on a metal having work function of 1.25 eV, placed in a magnetic field of intensity B. The electrons emitted perpendicular to the magnetic field B, with maximum kinetic energy are bent into a circular arc of radius 30 cm. The value of B is ________×10-7 T.
Given hc=20×10-26 J m, the mass of the electron=9×10-31 kg.
solution:
By photoelectric equation

hcλ-ϕ=kmax

kmax=1240500-1.25≈1.25

r=2mkeB

B=2mker

=125×10-7 T
